title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Gratuity & its Fund Management
Overview of Gratuity
By definition, gratuity is an additional benefit paid to someone as a gesture of appreciation for their services. In the tax parlance, gratuity is an additional benefit paid by the employer to the employee for the latter’s efforts towards the organisation. Gratuity is paid if the employee has worked for a minimum of five years1 for the employer. It is paid as a lump sum benefit to the employee. This is the gratuity meaning.
Gratuity in India
In India, the payment of gratuity is governed under the Payment of Gratuity Act of 19721. The Act is applicable for employees working in mines, factories, ports, plantations, oilfields, shops, companies or any other establishment that employs a minimum of 10 employees2.
Gratuity payment is a statutory requirement for employers2. Employers can either fund gratuity payments from the business funds or buy a group gratuity insurance policy to fund the gratuity payments easily. This is the simplest way to understand what is gratuity.
How Does Gratuity Work?
Employers covered under the Payment of Gratuity Act of 1972 are required to pay gratuities to eligible employees. If the employees fulfil the eligibility criteria for gratuity payment, they will receive the gratuity benefit in either of the following cases3 –
When they retire
On their resignation or termination of service. However, if the employee is terminated on the grounds of bad conduct, the gratuity payment can be withheld or rejected.
When the employee dies
When the employee becomes disabled due to an illness or an accident
Also, in the case of death and disablement, the requirement of completing five years of continuous service is not applicable3. Employees who have served for a lower tenure also become eligible for gratuity payments. You can understand the workings of gratuity only when you know the gratuity meaning.
Gratuity Eligibility
Employees would be able to receive gratuity from the employer provided the following criteria are fulfilled1 –
The employee is eligible for superannuation
The employee is retiring from service
The employee is resigning from the job after completing 5 years of continuous and uninterrupted service
If the employee becomes disabled due to an illness or an accident
If the employee dies whilst in employment.
How Is Gratuity Calculated?
To understand what is gratuity amount, the calculation can be done with a formula which has been prescribed under the Payment of Gratuity Act of 1972. The formula uses two important parameters for gratuity calculation – the number of years worked and the last drawn salary and understand what is gratuity.
The number of years worked is taken as a whole number. If you have worked for a fraction of a year, it is rounded off to the nearest year. For instance, if you have worked for 7 years and 8 months, the number of years would be considered as 8 years. On the other hand, if you have worked for 7 years and 5 months, the number of years would be considered as 7 years. 6 months is taken as the mid-point for rounding off. If the number of months exceeds 6, the next year is considered for calculation and if the number of months is lower than 6, the number of months is not considered. Instead, only the number of years is taken into consideration.
The last drawn salary means the basic pay plus the dearness allowance.
Moreover, gratuity calculation also depends on two other parameters – employees covered under the Payment of Gratuity Act of 1972 and those not covered under it.
Here are the formulae for the calculation of gratuity in both cases1 –
Employees covered under the Act
Gratuity = tenure of service X last drawn salary X 15/26
For instance, if an employee is employed for 8 years and 5 months with a last drawn salary of ₹50,000 per month, the gratuity would be calculated as follows –
8 X ₹50,000 X 15/26 = ₹2,30,769.
Employees not covered under the Act
Gratuity = tenure of service X last drawn salary X 15/30
In the same example as above, the gratuity would be calculated as follows –
Gratuity = 8 X ₹50,000 X 15/30 = ₹2,00,000.
If the employee dies, gratuity calculation is done as a multiple of the last drawn salary and the multiple depends on the service tenure. Here’s how gratuity is calculated on death2-
The maximum gratuity payment allowed under the Act is limited to ₹20 lakhs2. If the employer pays a gratuity higher than this amount, the excess would be treated as an ex-gratia payment and would be at the discretion of the employer2.
Tax Rules & Regulations on Gratuity
Gratuity is taxed in the hands of the employee depending on his employment. If the employee is employed in any government organisation, the gratuity amount received would be completely tax-exempt1.
For non-government employees or employees working in the private sector, tax exemption is available on the amount of gratuity received. Exemption is available on the lowest of the following three values1 if the employer is covered under the Payment of Gratuity Act of 1972–
₹20 lakhs
The actual gratuity received by the employee
15 days salary based on salary last drawn for each completed year of service or part thereof in excess of 6 months.
For instance, if an employee is employed for 6 years and 5 months with a last drawn salary of ₹40,000 per month, the gratuity would be calculated as follows if the employer is covered under the Payment of Gratuity Act of 1972 –
6 X ₹40,000 X 15/26 = ₹1,38,462
His employer, suppose, pays him a gratuity of ₹5 lakhs. In this case, the lowest of the three amounts (₹20 lakhs, ₹5 lakhs and ₹1,38,62) is ₹1,38,462. As such, the exemption would be allowed on this amount. The rest ₹3,61,538 (₹5,00,000 - ₹1,38,462)would be taxable.
If the employer is not covered under the Act, tax exemption is allowed on the lowest of the following three values4 –
Half month’s basic salary for each completed year of service
₹20 lakhs
The actual amount of gratuity received
For instance, in the above example, if the employer is not covered under the Act, the tax-free value of gratuity would be the lowest of the following –
Service tenure X half month’s basic salary = 6 X ₹20,000 = ₹1,20,000
₹20 lakhs
₹5 lakhs
Here, the employee can claim tax exemption on ₹1,20,000 while the remaining ₹3,80,000 (gratuity received less the exempted amount) would be taxable.
Facts About Gratuity
Here are some facts about gratuity that you should know –
Ex-gratia gratuity payments are taxed in employees’ hands at their income tax slab rates.
While you can calculate gratuity using the aforementioned formulae, there are online gratuity calculators for quick calculations
In the case of the employee’s death, gratuity is paid to the nominee or legal heirs1
Contractual employees, not under the payroll of the employer, are not eligible for gratuity2
Gratuity which is not tax-exempt is taxed at your income tax slab rates
Conclusion
If you are a salaried employee and have completed 5 years of continuous service, understand gratuity meaning and how it is calculated. Know how much gratuity you are eligible to get so that you can plan for your financial goals. Also, nominate a family member to receive the gratuity benefit in the case of your premature demise. Know the tax rules for gratuity too so that you can plan your taxes and claim exemption on the eligible amount.
FAQs
What kind of employees does the Gratuity Act 1972 cover?
The Gratuity Act of 1972 covers employees working in companies, mines, factories, ports, plantations, oilfields, shops, or any other establishment whether owned privately or by the government2.
What is the time frame to release the payment of Gratuity from an employer?
Per specified rules, employers are required to pay the gratuity amount within 30 days from the date of full and final settlement of the employee’s dues. If the employer delays the payment, it would have to bear additional interest charges on the outstanding gratuity amount2.
Is there any upper limit to the gratuity amount?
Yes, the maximum gratuity allowed under the Gratuity Act of 1972 is ₹20 lakhs2.
Do I lose my gratuity amount if the employer goes bankrupt?
Gratuity is a mandatory payment that the employer is required to make to eligible employees. So, even if the employer files for bankruptcy, gratuity payments would be made1.
